title: Unni Krishnan J.P. v. State of Andhra Pradesh
legal_principles: Right to education as fundamental right
summary: The Supreme Court held that right to education up to age 14 is a fundamental right under Article 21, read with Articles 41 and 45 of the Directive Principles. It also laid down a scheme (fee structure) for private engineering and medical colleges. Later, the 86th Constitutional Amendment (2002) gave explicit recognition to right to education, leading to the Right to Education Act 2009.
key_holdings: Right to education up to age 14 is a fundamental right under Article 21
State must provide free and compulsory education up to 14 years
Private educational institutions can charge fees but not capitation fees (profiteering prohibited)
Education is not a business; it is a charitable activity
key_quotes: Right to education flows directly from right to life.
Capitation fee amounts to commercialisation of education which is opposed to our constitutional scheme.
why_important: Recognised right to education as fundamental right — paved way for 86th Amendment and Right to Education Act 2009. Regulated capitation fees in private colleges.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to education-related tax exemptions (Section 10 of Income Tax Act) and compliance requirements for educational institutions.
